AVRT is the company that makes the sensors.
The founder who started it is Izabel.
Izabel is a CEO whose background is in electronics.
AVRT, which was founded in 2015, is growing fast.
AVRT, which was founded in 2015, is growing.
The city where AVRT is based is Valinhos.
Izabel, I am briefing a new board member tomorrow. In a few sentences, how would you describe AVRT?
AVRT, which I founded in Valinhos in 2015, is a company that designs wireless sensors. Our flagship product, the sensor that made our name, is used by factories that track equipment in real time.
Good. And who are the key people they should know?
The engineer who leads research, and whose designs won two awards, built the team. And there is a mentor who advised me early on and still shapes how we think.
And the market? Where does AVRT actually compete?
We serve a niche which most large firms ignore: clients who need cutting-edge sensors and the proprietary software that runs them. The headquarters where we operate sits just outside Valinhos.
And the competition? Who is the one to watch?

The incumbent that once dominated the segment, a firm whose prices were always lower, is the one we are catching. We recently opened a subsidiary, which handles North American sales, and our track record is what clients trust.
AVRT is the company that makes wireless sensors.
We serve a niche which large firms ignore.
AVRT, which was founded in 2015, is catching the incumbent.

Hi Izabel, it is Philip, the investor who backed your first round. I am briefing a partner about AVRT, and I want to get the story right, so tell me if I am wrong. AVRT, which you founded in Valinhos back in 2015, is the company that makes the wireless sensors factories use to track equipment. You are the CEO whose background is in electronics, and the engineer who leads your research is the one whose designs won those two awards. What I like is the niche, which most big firms ignore: clients who need cutting-edge sensors and the proprietary software that runs them. The subsidiary that you opened this year, the one which handles North American sales, is the piece that got my partner's attention. A company that owns its technology and has a track record clients trust is exactly the kind of venture we want. Call me.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Paula, your market analyst, with a quick read on the competition. The segment you sell into, which is growing about fifteen percent a year, used to be dominated by one incumbent, the firm whose prices were always lower than yours. That is the company that everyone assumed would win. But the buyers who matter, the ones who run large factories, care about reliability, not just price. AVRT, which built its name on quality, is now the supplier they call first. Your flagship sensor, the product that started it all, is still the benchmark that rivals are measured against. My advice, which I will put in the note I circulate, is simple: keep telling the story of the mentor who guided you and the track record that clients trust. That is the message which sells. Talk soon.
